questions.question: What is the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)?
questions.answer:
SRP states that a class should have only one reason to change — it should do one thing and do it well. "One reason to change" is more precise than "one thing": a class has one responsibility, so only one type of requirement change should require modifying it.

Violation example:

// BAD: Three reasons to change (data access, business logic, email)
public class InvoiceService
{
    public void ProcessInvoice(Invoice invoice)
    {
        // 1. DB access
        _db.SaveInvoice(invoice);

        // 2. Business logic
        if (invoice.Amount > 10000) ApplyDiscount(invoice);

        // 3. Email sending
        _smtp.SendEmail(invoice.ClientEmail, "Invoice generated");
    }
}

Fixed:

// Three classes, each with one responsibility
public class InvoiceRepository    { void Save(Invoice i) { ... } }
public class InvoicePricingService { void ApplyDiscount(Invoice i) { ... } }
public class InvoiceNotifier     { Task SendReceiptAsync(Invoice i) { ... } }

public class InvoiceProcessor  // orchestrates, doesn't do the work itself
{
    public async Task ProcessAsync(Invoice invoice)
    {
        _repo.Save(invoice);
        _pricing.ApplyDiscount(invoice);
        await _notifier.SendReceiptAsync(invoice);
    }
}

In the PaymentReminder project, I violated SRP initially by having a single Worker class that fetched invoices, built emails, and called SMTP. Splitting into InvoiceFetcher, EmailBuilder, and EmailSender made each unit independently testable and easier to maintain.
followUps: How does SRP relate to cohesion?
How do you know when a class violates SRP?

followUpAnswers.How does SRP relate to cohesion?:
SRP and cohesion are two sides of the same coin. Cohesion is a metric that measures how closely related the responsibilities of a module or class are. A highly cohesive class has members that all work toward a single, well-defined purpose. SRP is the design guideline that produces high cohesion as an outcome.

When a class violates SRP — say, an InvoiceService that does data access, pricing logic, and email sending — its members are loosely cohesive: they serve different masters and change for different reasons. When you enforce SRP by splitting that class into InvoiceRepository, InvoicePricingService, and InvoiceNotifier, each resulting class has high cohesion because all its methods relate to one purpose.

The relationship can be stated precisely:

  • High cohesion = all methods/properties in the class work toward one purpose = SRP satisfied
  • Low cohesion = methods serve unrelated purposes = SRP violated

Robert C. Martin defines a "responsibility" as a reason to change, and a reason to change maps directly to a stakeholder or actor. If two different teams (the DBA team and the email team) could independently demand changes to your class, it serves two actors — it has low cohesion and violates SRP.

// Low cohesion — three actors can demand changes to this one class
public class ReportService
{
    public Report GenerateReport(int id) { ... }  // Finance team owns this
    public void PrintReport(Report r) { ... }      // IT/Print team owns this
    public void SaveToDatabase(Report r) { ... }   // DBA team owns this
}

// High cohesion — one actor per class, SRP satisfied
public class ReportGenerator { public Report Generate(int id) { ... } }
public class ReportPrinter   { public void Print(Report r) { ... } }
public class ReportRepository { public void Save(Report r) { ... } }

The practical implication: if you can't describe what a class does in a single sentence without using "and", it likely has low cohesion and violates SRP.
followUpAnswers.How do you know when a class violates SR:
Several concrete signals indicate an SRP violation:

  • The "and" test — if describing the class requires the word "and" ("it validates invoices and persists them and sends emails"), each "and" is a separate responsibility.
  • Multiple reasons to change — ask: who would request a change to this class? If the answer involves multiple different stakeholders (the DBA team, the email team, the finance team), you have multiple responsibilities.
  • Huge class size — a class with 500+ lines or 20+ methods is a strong smell. Focused classes are naturally small.
  • Difficult unit testing — if setting up a unit test requires mocking three or four unrelated dependencies, the class does too much.
  • Frequent merge conflicts — if multiple developers routinely modify the same file for unrelated features, the class serves too many masters.
  • Unrelated imports/using directives — a class that imports both System.Data.SqlClient and System.Net.Mail and Newtonsoft.Json is almost certainly doing too many things.
// All of these warning signs in one class:
public class UserManager
{
    // DB access (DBA team cares)
    private readonly SqlConnection _conn;
    public User GetById(int id) { ... }
    public void Save(User u) { ... }

    // Email (comms team cares)
    private readonly SmtpClient _smtp;
    public void SendWelcomeEmail(User u) { ... }

    // Password hashing (security team cares)
    public string HashPassword(string pwd) { ... }

    // Report generation (finance team cares)
    public byte[] ExportUserReport() { ... }
}

// Fix: split by actor/reason to change
public class UserRepository    { ... }  // DBA team's concern
public class UserEmailService   { ... }  // Comms team's concern
public class PasswordHasher     { ... }  // Security team's concern
public class UserReportExporter { ... }  // Finance team's concern

Practical approach: Before writing a new method, ask yourself: "Does this belong to the same actor (stakeholder) as the existing methods?" If not, it belongs in a different class.

questions.tip: Red flag for SRP violation: If describing the class's purpose requires the word "and" — "it saves invoices AND sends emails AND applies discounts" — it violates SRP. Each "and" is a separate responsibility.

questions.question: What is the Open/Closed Principle (OCP)?
questions.answer:
OCP states that classes should be open for extension but closed for modification. You should be able to add new behavior without changing existing, tested code.

Violation: adding a new notification type requires modifying existing code:

// BAD: every new notification type requires modifying this method
public void SendNotification(string type, string message)
{
    if (type == "email")  SendEmail(message);
    else if (type == "sms")  SendSms(message);
    else if (type == "push")  SendPush(message); // had to add this
}

Fixed with polymorphism:

// GOOD: add new types by adding new classes, not modifying existing ones
public interface INotificationSender
{
    string Type { get; }
    Task SendAsync(string message, CancellationToken ct);
}

public class EmailSender : INotificationSender { ... }
public class SmsSender : INotificationSender { ... }
public class PushSender : INotificationSender { ... }  // NEW: no existing code changes

public class NotificationRouter
{
    private readonly IEnumerable<INotificationSender> _senders;

    public async Task SendAsync(string type, string message, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        var sender = _senders.Single(s => s.Type == type);
        await sender.SendAsync(message, ct);
    }
}

In the PaymentReminder service I used this pattern for notification channels. Adding WhatsApp notifications later required only a new WhatsAppSender class — zero changes to existing code.
followUps: How do you implement OCP using the Strategy pattern?
Is OCP realistic — don't you always have to modify something?

followUpAnswers.How do you implement OCP using the Strat:
The Strategy pattern is one of the most direct implementations of OCP. You define a family of algorithms (strategies) behind a common interface. The context class depends on the interface — not on any concrete strategy. Adding a new strategy means adding a new class; the context code never changes.

Step-by-step in C#:

// 1. Define the strategy interface
public interface IDiscountStrategy
{
    decimal Apply(decimal price, int quantity);
}

// 2. Concrete strategies — adding a new one never touches existing code
public class NoDiscount : IDiscountStrategy
{
    public decimal Apply(decimal price, int quantity) => price * quantity;
}

public class BulkDiscount : IDiscountStrategy
{
    public decimal Apply(decimal price, int quantity)
        => quantity >= 10 ? price * quantity * 0.85m : price * quantity;
}

public class SeasonalDiscount : IDiscountStrategy  // NEW — zero changes elsewhere
{
    private readonly decimal _rate;
    public SeasonalDiscount(decimal rate) => _rate = rate;
    public decimal Apply(decimal price, int quantity)
        => price * quantity * (1 - _rate);
}

// 3. Context depends on the abstraction
public class OrderCalculator
{
    private readonly IDiscountStrategy _strategy;

    public OrderCalculator(IDiscountStrategy strategy)
        => _strategy = strategy;

    public decimal CalculateTotal(decimal unitPrice, int qty)
        => _strategy.Apply(unitPrice, qty);
}

// 4. Wiring — in ASP.NET Core DI
builder.Services.AddKeyedScoped<IDiscountStrategy, NoDiscount>("none");
builder.Services.AddKeyedScoped<IDiscountStrategy, BulkDiscount>("bulk");
builder.Services.AddKeyedScoped<IDiscountStrategy, SeasonalDiscount>("seasonal");

The key insight is that the OrderCalculator context is closed for modification — its source code never changes when a new discount type is introduced. It is open for extension because you extend its behavior by injecting a different strategy.

In ASP.NET Core, the DI container with named/keyed services or IEnumerable<IDiscountStrategy> registration makes runtime strategy selection clean and testable.
followUpAnswers.Is OCP realistic — don't you always have:
This is an excellent critical question, and the honest answer is: OCP is an ideal, not an absolute law. You always have to modify something when requirements change. What OCP says is that the modification should be confined to extension points and wiring code (registrations, factories, configuration) — not to your core business logic classes.

What you DO modify when following OCP:

  • You add a new class (the extension)
  • You update the DI registrations in Program.cs or a module
  • You may update a configuration file or database record

What you do NOT modify:

  • The existing tested business logic classes
  • The interface definition (unless the design was wrong)
  • The consumer/context that depends on the abstraction
// When you add a new payment method:
// MODIFY: Program.cs (add registration) — this is expected
builder.Services.AddScoped<IPaymentProcessor, CryptoPaymentProcessor>();

// DO NOT MODIFY:
// - CreditCardPaymentProcessor (tested, working)
// - PayPalPaymentProcessor (tested, working)
// - CheckoutService (depends on IPaymentProcessor)
// - IPaymentProcessor interface

When OCP is NOT worth applying:

  • When the class is unlikely to ever be extended (YAGNI — You Aren't Gonna Need It)
  • When premature abstraction would add complexity with no benefit
  • When you're in the early exploratory phase of a feature

Martin Fowler's advice: don't apply OCP everywhere upfront. Let the first change teach you where the extension point should be, then refactor to close the class against future changes of the same kind. This is the "Rule of Three" — after three similar changes to the same code, abstract it.

questions.tip: OCP is about anticipated change points. You can't make everything extensible without modification. Identify where requirements are likely to change (notification types, payment methods, export formats) and design those extension points. The rest can be closed.

questions.question: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)?
questions.answer:
LSP states that if S is a subtype of T, then anywhere you use T, you can substitute S without breaking the program. Subclasses must honor the contract of the base class.

Classic violation — the Square/Rectangle problem:

public class Rectangle
{
    public virtual int Width { get; set; }
    public virtual int Height { get; set; }
    public int Area() => Width * Height;
}

public class Square : Rectangle  // LSP violation!
{
    public override int Width  { set { base.Width = base.Height = value; } }
    public override int Height { set { base.Width = base.Height = value; } }
}

// Consumer that expects Rectangle contract is broken:
void ResizeAndCalculate(Rectangle r)
{
    r.Width = 5;
    r.Height = 10;
    Assert(r.Area() == 50);  // FAILS for Square: area is 100!
}

Fix: Don't use inheritance here — Square and Rectangle are not substitutable. Use separate classes or an IShape interface.

Practical LSP checks:

  • Derived class should not throw exceptions the base doesn't
  • Derived class should not strengthen preconditions or weaken postconditions
  • Derived class should not remove behaviors the base provides
followUps: What is the precondition/postcondition rule in LSP?
How does LSP relate to interface segregation?

followUpAnswers.What is the precondition/postcondition r:
The precondition/postcondition rule comes from Bertrand Meyer's Design by Contract, which Barbara Liskov formalized into LSP. The rules are:

  • Preconditions (what must be true before a method is called) — a subtype must accept at least as much as the base type. Subclasses cannot strengthen preconditions (they cannot be more restrictive than the base).
  • Postconditions (what is guaranteed after a method completes) — a subtype must guarantee at least as much as the base type. Subclasses cannot weaken postconditions (they cannot deliver less than the base promises).
public class BankAccount
{
    // Precondition: amount > 0
    // Postcondition: Balance increases by amount
    public virtual void Deposit(decimal amount)
    {
        if (amount <= 0) throw new ArgumentException("amount must be > 0");
        Balance += amount;
    }

    // Precondition: amount > 0 AND amount <= Balance
    // Postcondition: Balance decreases by amount
    public virtual void Withdraw(decimal amount)
    {
        if (amount <= 0 || amount > Balance)
            throw new InvalidOperationException();
        Balance -= amount;
    }
}

// LSP VIOLATION — Premium account strengthens precondition on Deposit
public class PremiumAccount : BankAccount
{
    public override void Deposit(decimal amount)
    {
        // Now requires amount >= 100 — STRONGER precondition!
        if (amount < 100) throw new ArgumentException("Min deposit is 100");
        base.Deposit(amount);
    }
}

// Consumer code breaks when PremiumAccount is substituted:
BankAccount account = new PremiumAccount();
account.Deposit(50);  // Was valid for BankAccount, throws for PremiumAccount — LSP violated!

// LSP VIOLATION — ReadOnlyAccount weakens postcondition on Withdraw
public class ReadOnlyAccount : BankAccount
{
    public override void Withdraw(decimal amount)
    {
        // Does nothing — postcondition (balance decreases) is not honored!
    }
}

Memory rule:

  • Preconditions: subclass must be equal or more permissive (accept the same or more)
  • Postconditions: subclass must be equal or stronger (deliver the same or more)

In C#, the compiler enforces some of this (you can't override a method and throw a new checked exception), but logical contract violations like the examples above require code review and discipline to catch. Tools like Contracts or documentation of expected behavior help enforce these rules.
followUpAnswers.How does LSP relate to interface segrega:
LSP and ISP (Interface Segregation Principle) are closely related — ISP prevents many LSP violations before they happen.

When an interface is too fat (too many methods), implementers are forced to provide implementations for methods that don't apply to them. The typical response is to throw NotImplementedException or NotSupportedException — which is an LSP violation because callers using the interface cannot substitute the concrete type without unexpected failures.

// FAT interface — forces LSP violations in implementers
public interface IFileStorage
{
    Task<byte[]> ReadAsync(string path);
    Task WriteAsync(string path, byte[] data);
    Task DeleteAsync(string path);      // read-only storage can't do this!
    Task MoveAsync(string src, string dst); // same problem
    Task<long> GetSizeAsync(string path);
}

// LSP violation caused by fat interface:
public class ReadOnlyArchiveStorage : IFileStorage
{
    public Task<byte[]> ReadAsync(string path) { /* real impl */ }
    public Task<long> GetSizeAsync(string path) { /* real impl */ }

    // Forced to implement but semantically wrong — LSP violated!
    public Task WriteAsync(string path, byte[] data)
        => throw new NotSupportedException();
    public Task DeleteAsync(string path)
        => throw new NotSupportedException();
    public Task MoveAsync(string src, string dst)
        => throw new NotSupportedException();
}

// Caller breaks when ReadOnlyArchiveStorage is substituted:
IFileStorage storage = GetStorage();  // might return ReadOnlyArchiveStorage
await storage.DeleteAsync("/important/file");  // throws unexpectedly!

// FIX: Apply ISP to prevent the LSP violation
public interface IReadableStorage  { Task<byte[]> ReadAsync(string path); Task<long> GetSizeAsync(string path); }
public interface IWritableStorage  { Task WriteAsync(string path, byte[] data); }
public interface IDeletableStorage { Task DeleteAsync(string path); Task MoveAsync(string src, string dst); }

// Now ReadOnlyArchiveStorage only implements what it truly supports
public class ReadOnlyArchiveStorage : IReadableStorage
{
    public Task<byte[]> ReadAsync(string path) { ... }
    public Task<long> GetSizeAsync(string path) { ... }
    // No write/delete methods — can't be substituted where write is expected
}

The relationship summarized: ISP splits fat interfaces into focused ones, ensuring each implementer only commits to behaviors it can genuinely fulfill. This prevents the "throw NotImplementedException" pattern that is the most common LSP violation in practice. Following ISP makes LSP easier to satisfy automatically.

questions.tip: LSP in practice: If you find yourself checking if (obj is DerivedClass) before calling methods, you're violating LSP — the derived class isn't truly substitutable. Refactor to polymorphism instead of type checks.

questions.question: What is the Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)?
questions.answer:
ISP states that clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they don't use. Fat interfaces should be split into smaller, more focused ones.

Violation — fat interface:

// BAD: every implementer must implement methods they don't need
public interface IWorker
{
    void Work();
    void Eat();  // robots don't eat!
    void Sleep(); // robots don't sleep!
}

public class RobotWorker : IWorker
{
    public void Work() { /* actual logic */ }
    public void Eat() => throw new NotImplementedException();  // forced!
    public void Sleep() => throw new NotImplementedException(); // forced!
}

Fixed — segregated interfaces:

public interface IWorkable  { void Work(); }
public interface IFeedable  { void Eat(); }
public interface ISleepable { void Sleep(); }

public class HumanWorker : IWorkable, IFeedable, ISleepable
{
    public void Work()  { ... }
    public void Eat()   { ... }
    public void Sleep() { ... }
}

public class RobotWorker : IWorkable  // only what it needs
{
    public void Work() { ... }
}

In my services layer: instead of one IUserService with 20 methods, I have IUserAuthService, IUserProfileService, and IUserNotificationService. A controller that only needs auth doesn't depend on profile or notification methods.
followUps: How does ISP relate to cohesion?

followUpAnswers.How does ISP relate to cohesion?:
ISP and cohesion describe the same desirable property from different angles. ISP is the design rule; cohesion is the quality metric that results from following it.

A fat interface has low cohesion: its methods serve different clients and change for different reasons. Splitting it per ISP produces small interfaces where every method is used by every client — that is the definition of high cohesion.

There are different levels of cohesion (from worst to best): coincidental, logical, temporal, procedural, communicational, sequential, and functional. ISP pushes interfaces toward functional cohesion — every member serves one and only one purpose, and all members contribute to that same purpose.

// Low cohesion interface — methods serve three different clients
public interface IOrderService
{
    // Used by checkout controller
    Task PlaceOrderAsync(Cart cart);
    Task CancelOrderAsync(int orderId);

    // Used by reporting service only
    Task<IEnumerable<Order>> GetAllOrdersForPeriodAsync(DateRange range);
    Task<decimal> GetRevenueSummaryAsync(DateRange range);

    // Used by warehouse service only
    Task MarkOrderShippedAsync(int orderId, string trackingCode);
    Task MarkOrderDeliveredAsync(int orderId);
}

// High cohesion — each interface serves one client, one purpose
public interface IOrderManagement
{
    Task PlaceOrderAsync(Cart cart);
    Task CancelOrderAsync(int orderId);
}

public interface IOrderReporting
{
    Task<IEnumerable<Order>> GetAllOrdersForPeriodAsync(DateRange range);
    Task<decimal> GetRevenueSummaryAsync(DateRange range);
}

public interface IOrderFulfillment
{
    Task MarkOrderShippedAsync(int orderId, string trackingCode);
    Task MarkOrderDeliveredAsync(int orderId);
}

The practical benefit: high-cohesion interfaces are easier to mock in tests, easier to understand, and have fewer transitive dependencies. A test for the checkout controller only needs to mock IOrderManagement — not six unrelated methods.
followUpAnswers.How fine-grained should interfaces be?:
This is a judgment call, and over-segregation is a real anti-pattern just as under-segregation is. The guiding principle is: interfaces should be scoped to the needs of their clients, not to individual methods.

Too coarse (fat interface):

// Bad: 15 methods, clients only use 2-3 each
public interface IUserService
{
    Task<User> GetByIdAsync(int id);
    Task<User> GetByEmailAsync(string email);
    Task CreateAsync(User user);
    Task UpdateAsync(User user);
    Task DeleteAsync(int id);
    Task<bool> ValidatePasswordAsync(int id, string pwd);
    Task ChangePasswordAsync(int id, string newPwd);
    Task SendWelcomeEmailAsync(int id);
    Task SendPasswordResetAsync(string email);
    Task<IEnumerable<User>> SearchAsync(string query);
    // ... 5 more ...
}

Too fine-grained (ISP taken too far):

// Bad: one interface per method — absurd granularity
public interface IUserGetter   { Task<User> GetByIdAsync(int id); }
public interface IUserCreator  { Task CreateAsync(User user); }
public interface IUserUpdater  { Task UpdateAsync(User user); }
public interface IUserDeleter  { Task DeleteAsync(int id); }
// Mocking 5 interfaces per test is worse than having one reasonable interface

Right level — grouped by client/use case:

// Client 1: authentication middleware
public interface IUserAuthService
{
    Task<User?> GetByEmailAsync(string email);
    Task<bool> ValidatePasswordAsync(int id, string pwd);
    Task ChangePasswordAsync(int id, string newPwd);
}

// Client 2: admin CRUD controllers
public interface IUserAdminService
{
    Task<User> GetByIdAsync(int id);
    Task CreateAsync(User user);
    Task UpdateAsync(User user);
    Task DeleteAsync(int id);
    Task<IEnumerable<User>> SearchAsync(string query);
}

// Client 3: notification/email system
public interface IUserNotificationService
{
    Task SendWelcomeEmailAsync(int id);
    Task SendPasswordResetAsync(string email);
}

Rules of thumb:

  • Group methods that change together and are used together
  • If only one client ever calls a method, that method belongs in an interface scoped to that client
  • A single-method interface (role interface) is fine when it models a true single behavior (e.g., IDisposable, IComparable)
  • Stop splitting when the interface is already easy to mock, easy to describe, and only one type of client uses it

questions.tip: NotImplementedException is a red flag for ISP violation. If an implementer throws NotImplementedException for interface methods, the interface is too broad and should be split.

questions.question: What is the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)?
questions.answer:
DIP has two rules:

  1. High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on abstractions.
  2. Abstractions should not depend on details. Details (implementations) should depend on abstractions.

Without DIP:

// BAD: high-level InvoiceService directly depends on low-level EmailSender
public class InvoiceService
{
    private readonly SmtpEmailSender _emailSender = new SmtpEmailSender();  // concrete!

    public async Task CreateInvoiceAsync(Invoice invoice)
    {
        await _db.SaveAsync(invoice);
        await _emailSender.SendAsync(invoice.ClientEmail, "Invoice created");
        // Can't swap to SendGrid without changing InvoiceService
    }
}

With DIP:

// GOOD: both depend on the abstraction IEmailSender
public class InvoiceService
{
    private readonly IEmailSender _emailSender;  // abstraction

    public InvoiceService(IEmailSender emailSender)  // injected
        => _emailSender = emailSender;

    public async Task CreateInvoiceAsync(Invoice invoice)
    {
        await _db.SaveAsync(invoice);
        await _emailSender.SendAsync(invoice.ClientEmail, "Invoice created");
        // Swap SmtpEmailSender → SendGridEmailSender: zero changes here
    }
}

// In tests: inject FakeEmailSender — no SMTP needed

DIP is what makes Dependency Injection work. The DI container wires up the concrete implementations; your business code never sees them.
followUps: How does DIP relate to DI (Dependency Injection)?

followUpAnswers.How does DIP relate to DI (Dependency In:
DIP is the principle; DI is the most common mechanism used to implement it. Understanding the distinction is important in interviews.

DIP (the principle) says: high-level modules should depend on abstractions, not concretions. It says nothing about how those abstractions get their concrete implementations — that's an implementation detail.

DI (Dependency Injection) is a technique where a class receives its dependencies from the outside (injected by a caller or a container) rather than creating them itself. Constructor injection is the most common form in .NET.

DI implements DIP by ensuring that when a class declares a dependency on IEmailSender, it never constructs a concrete SmtpEmailSender — the concrete is wired up externally.

// DIP without DI — satisfies DIP but wires manually
public class OrderService
{
    private readonly IEmailSender _sender;

    // DIP: depends on abstraction
    public OrderService(IEmailSender sender) => _sender = sender;
}

// Manual DI composition root (no container)
var orderService = new OrderService(
    new SmtpEmailSender(config["Smtp:Host"]));

// DI Container (ASP.NET Core) — same principle, automated
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>();
builder.Services.AddScoped<OrderService>();
// Container resolves IEmailSender → SmtpEmailSender automatically

Other ways to satisfy DIP without DI:

  • Factory pattern — IEmailSenderFactory.Create() returns an abstraction
  • Service Locator — the class calls a registry to get the dependency (works but is considered an anti-pattern — the dependency is hidden)
  • Abstract factory via static — like LogManager.GetLogger()

Constructor injection (DI) is preferred because dependencies are explicit (visible in the constructor signature), making the class easier to test and understand.
followUpAnswers.What is inversion of control (IoC)?:
IoC (Inversion of Control) is a broad design principle where the control flow of a program is inverted: instead of your code calling a framework, the framework calls your code. DI is one specific implementation of IoC; there are others.

Traditional control flow (no IoC):

// Your code is in charge of everything — it creates objects, calls them, decides order
class Program
{
    static void Main()
    {
        var db = new SqlDatabase("connection string");
        var emailSender = new SmtpEmailSender("smtp.gmail.com");
        var service = new OrderService(db, emailSender);
        service.ProcessOrder(new Order());
    }
}

IoC — control handed to the framework/container:

// You register types; the container decides how to create and inject them
builder.Services.AddScoped<IDatabase, SqlDatabase>();
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>();
builder.Services.AddScoped<OrderService>();

// ASP.NET Core calls YOUR code (controller actions) — not the other way around
// The framework controls the lifetime, creation, and injection of dependencies
[ApiController]
public class OrderController : ControllerBase
{
    // Framework injects this — you didn't create it
    public OrderController(OrderService svc) { ... }

    [HttpPost]
    public IActionResult Post(Order order) // framework calls this action
        => Ok(_svc.ProcessOrder(order));
}

IoC implementations beyond DI:

  • Event-driven / callbacks — you register a handler; the framework calls it when an event fires (e.g., button click handlers, app.Use(middleware))
  • Template Method pattern — base class controls algorithm flow; subclass overrides steps
  • Service Locator — class asks a registry for its dependencies at runtime (IoC but not recommended)

Summary relationship: IoC is the broad concept (control is inverted to a framework or container). DI is the most popular, clean implementation of IoC. DIP is the design principle that motivates using DI — depend on abstractions so implementations can be swapped by whoever controls the composition.

questions.tip: DIP ≠ DI. DIP is the principle (depend on abstractions). DI is one mechanism to achieve it. You could satisfy DIP with the Service Locator pattern too (though it's an anti-pattern). DI via constructor injection is the cleanest implementation of DIP.
